main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Chunk-Distilled Language Modeling (CD-LM): a training-free generation approach that combines language model (LM) token generation with chunk retrieval from a trie-structured datastore, using context matching in the LM's vector space to retrieve and inject multi-token text chunks during inference."],"problems":["Inefficiency of token-level generation in large language models (LLMs).","Difficulty of adapting LLMs to new data and knowledge without expensive retraining."],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Language modeling perplexity","Text generation quality","Inference efficiency"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a training-free, chunk-based retrieval-augmented language modeling approach that allows for multi-token generation in a single decoding step.","Enables flexible construction of datastores for chunk retrieval, supporting model-specific, domain-specific, or expert-curated knowledge injection without retraining.","Allows for both knowledge distillation (from larger to smaller models) and training-free domain adaptation by injecting memorized or novel chunks during inference.","Improves both efficiency (by skipping multiple token decoding steps) and adaptability of LLMs to new knowledge or domains."]}

methods: Chunk-Distilled Language Modeling (CD-LM): a training-free generation approach that combines language model (LM) token generation with chunk retrieval from a trie-structured datastore, using context matching in the LM's vector space to retrieve and inject multi-token text chunks during inference.
problems: Inefficiency of token-level generation in large language models (LLMs).
Difficulty of adapting LLMs to new data and knowledge without expensive retraining.
novelty_claims: Proposes a training-free, chunk-based retrieval-augmented language modeling approach that allows for multi-token generation in a single decoding step.
Enables flexible construction of datastores for chunk retrieval, supporting model-specific, domain-specific, or expert-curated knowledge injection without retraining.
Allows for both knowledge distillation (from larger to smaller models) and training-free domain adaptation by injecting memorized or novel chunks during inference.
Improves both efficiency (by skipping multiple token decoding steps) and adaptability of LLMs to new knowledge or domains.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["In-Context Retrieval-Augmented Language Modeling (In-Context RALM): prepending retrieved grounding documents to the input of an off-the-shelf language model without modifying the LM architecture or further training","Use of off-the-shelf general purpose retrievers (e.g., BM25) for document selection","Specialized document retrieval and ranking mechanisms for RALM, including zero-shot ranking with a small LM and a dedicated bidirectional reranker trained via self-supervision from the LM signal"],"problems":["Improving language modeling performance by grounding LMs in external knowledge","Mitigating factual inaccuracies and lack of source attribution in language model outputs","Reducing the complexity and deployment barriers of RALM systems that require LM architecture modifications and retraining"],"datasets":["WikiText-103","RealNews","ArXiv (from The Pile)","Stack Exchange (from The Pile)","FreeLaw (from The Pile)"],"metrics":["Perplexity"],"results":[{"metric":"Perplexity reduction (WikiText-103, GPT-2 345M with BM25 retriever)","value":"Outperforms GPT-2 762M"},{"metric":"Perplexity reduction (WikiText-103, GPT-2 345M with trained reranker)","value":"Outperforms GPT-2 1.5B"},{"metric":"Perplexity reduction (OPT 6.7B with off-the-shelf retriever)","value":"Matches OPT 66B"},{"metric":"Parameter efficiency","value":"In-Context RALM with off-the-shelf retriever yields gains equivalent to 2-3x increase in LM parameters; with reranker, an additional 2x increase"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a simple RALM framework (In-Context RALM) that requires no modification or retraining of the language model, only prepending retrieved documents to the input","Demonstrates that off-the-shelf retrievers can provide large language modeling gains when used in this framework","Introduces and evaluates specialized reranking methods (zero-shot LM ranking and self-supervised bidirectional reranker) for document selection in RALM, further boosting performance","Shows that In-Context RALM can achieve performance gains comparable to much larger LMs, enabling efficient and practical deployment even via API access","Provides a clean probe for developing and evaluating document retrieval methods specialized for language modeling"]}

methods: In-Context Retrieval-Augmented Language Modeling (In-Context RALM): prepending retrieved grounding documents to the input of an off-the-shelf language model without modifying the LM architecture or further training
Use of off-the-shelf general purpose retrievers (e.g., BM25) for document selection
Specialized document retrieval and ranking mechanisms for RALM, including zero-shot ranking with a small LM and a dedicated bidirectional reranker trained via self-supervision from the LM signal
problems: Improving language modeling performance by grounding LMs in external knowledge
Mitigating factual inaccuracies and lack of source attribution in language model outputs
Reducing the complexity and deployment barriers of RALM systems that require LM architecture modifications and retraining

results.metric: Perplexity reduction (WikiText-103, GPT-2 345M with BM25 retriever)
Perplexity reduction (WikiText-103, GPT-2 345M with trained reranker)

results.value: In-Context RALM with off-the-shelf retriever yields gains equivalent to 2-3x increase in LM parameters; with reranker, an additional 2x increase

novelty_claims: Proposes a simple RALM framework (In-Context RALM) that requires no modification or retraining of the language model, only prepending retrieved documents to the input
Demonstrates that off-the-shelf retrievers can provide large language modeling gains when used in this framework
Introduces and evaluates specialized reranking methods (zero-shot LM ranking and self-supervised bidirectional reranker) for document selection in RALM, further boosting performance
Shows that In-Context RALM can achieve performance gains comparable to much larger LMs, enabling efficient and practical deployment even via API access
Provides a clean probe for developing and evaluating document retrieval methods specialized for language modeling

methods: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) models combining pre-trained seq2seq transformer (BART) as parametric memory and a dense vector index of Wikipedia as non-parametric memory accessed via Dense Passage Retriever (DPR)
Two RAG formulations: one conditioning on the same retrieved passages for the whole sequence, and one allowing different passages per token
General-purpose fine-tuning approach for jointly training the retriever and generator end-to-end
problems: Improving knowledge access and manipulation in large pre-trained language models for knowledge-intensive NLP tasks
Providing provenance and updatability of world knowledge in language models
Enhancing performance on open-domain question answering, fact verification, and knowledge-intensive generation tasks
metrics: Fact, specificity, and diversity measures for generation tasks

results.value: Yes (exact numbers not specified in excerpt)
Yes (exact numbers not specified in excerpt)
Yes (exact numbers not specified in excerpt)
Within 4.3% of state-of-the-art pipeline models with strong retrieval supervision
Strongly outperforms recent approaches with specialized pre-training objectives (exact numbers not specified)
More factual, specific, and diverse than BART baseline

novelty_claims: First to bring hybrid parametric and non-parametric memory to general-purpose sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) models for generation, not just extractive tasks
Propose a general-purpose fine-tuning recipe for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) applicable to any seq2seq task
Demonstrate that unconstrained generation with RAG outperforms previous extractive approaches on open-domain QA
Show that non-parametric memory can be updated to reflect changes in world knowledge without retraining the whole model

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Self-Reflective Retrieval-Augmented Generation (Self-RAG) framework","Training LMs to generate and use reflection tokens (retrieval and critique tokens)","On-demand adaptive retrieval of passages","Self-critique and selection of best output segment based on factuality and quality","Customizable inference-time decoding using reflection tokens for segment-level beam search"],"problems":["Factual inaccuracies in LLM-generated responses due to reliance on parametric knowledge","Indiscriminate retrieval and incorporation of passages in RAG methods, leading to reduced versatility and low-quality generations","Lack of explicit mechanisms for LLMs to assess and improve their own outputs for factuality and relevance"],"datasets":["A diverse collection of text interleaved with reflection tokens and retrieved passages (specific datasets not named in the provided content)","Six tasks including open-domain QA, reasoning, fact verification, and long-form generation (exact datasets not specified in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Factuality","Citation accuracy","Overall generation quality","Task-specific performance (e.g., open-domain QA accuracy, reasoning task accuracy)"],"results":[{"metric":"Citation accuracy (long-form generation)","value":"Significant gains over ChatGPT and retrieval-augmented Llama2-chat"},{"metric":"Performance on open-domain QA, reasoning, and fact verification tasks","value":"Outperforms ChatGPT and retrieval-augmented Llama2-chat"},{"metric":"Performance on six diverse tasks","value":"Outperforms Llama2-chat and Alpaca on all tasks; outperforms retrieval-augmented ChatGPT on four tasks"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of Self-RAG, a framework that enables LMs to adaptively retrieve, generate, and critique through self-reflection using reflection tokens.","Training LMs to generate and use reflection tokens (retrieval and critique tokens) for on-demand retrieval and self-assessment of output quality.","Unified end-to-end training of LMs to interleave task output, retrieval, and self-critique, improving factuality and versatility.","Customizable inference-time decoding that leverages reflection tokens to adjust retrieval frequency and model behavior to user preferences.","Demonstrated significant improvements in factuality and citation accuracy over state-of-the-art LLMs and RAG models."]}

methods: Self-Reflective Retrieval-Augmented Generation (Self-RAG) framework
Training LMs to generate and use reflection tokens (retrieval and critique tokens)
On-demand adaptive retrieval of passages
Self-critique and selection of best output segment based on factuality and quality
Customizable inference-time decoding using reflection tokens for segment-level beam search
problems: Factual inaccuracies in LLM-generated responses due to reliance on parametric knowledge
Indiscriminate retrieval and incorporation of passages in RAG methods, leading to reduced versatility and low-quality generations
Lack of explicit mechanisms for LLMs to assess and improve their own outputs for factuality and relevance
datasets: A diverse collection of text interleaved with reflection tokens and retrieved passages (specific datasets not named in the provided content)
Six tasks including open-domain QA, reasoning, fact verification, and long-form generation (exact datasets not specified in the excerpt)
metrics: Task-specific performance (e.g., open-domain QA accuracy, reasoning task accuracy)

results.value: Significant gains over ChatGPT and retrieval-augmented Llama2-chat

results.metric: Performance on open-domain QA, reasoning, and fact verification tasks

results.value: Outperforms Llama2-chat and Alpaca on all tasks; outperforms retrieval-augmented ChatGPT on four tasks

novelty_claims: Introduction of Self-RAG, a framework that enables LMs to adaptively retrieve, generate, and critique through self-reflection using reflection tokens.
Training LMs to generate and use reflection tokens (retrieval and critique tokens) for on-demand retrieval and self-assessment of output quality.
Unified end-to-end training of LMs to interleave task output, retrieval, and self-critique, improving factuality and versatility.
Customizable inference-time decoding that leverages reflection tokens to adjust retrieval frequency and model behavior to user preferences.
Demonstrated significant improvements in factuality and citation accuracy over state-of-the-art LLMs and RAG models.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Nearest Neighbor Speculative Decoding (NEST)","Confidence-based interpolation using Relative Retrieval Confidence (RRC) score","Dynamic span selection inspired by Copy Generator (COG)","Relaxed speculative decoding with prefix acceptance"],"problems":["Hallucination in large language models (LLMs)","Lack of attribution in LLM generations","Slow inference speeds and non-fluent text in semi-parametric LMs like kNN-LM","Inefficiency and unreliable attribution in retrieval-augmented LMs"],"datasets":["WikiText-103","Biography","MMLU","Pile-of-Law","TruthfulQA"],"metrics":["ROUGE-1","FACTSCORE","Attribution rate","Inference speed (generation latency)"],"results":[{"metric":"ROUGE-1 (WikiText-103, Llama-2-Chat 70B + NEST)","value":"42.3% improvement over baseline"},{"metric":"FACTSCORE (Biography, Llama-2-Chat 70B + NEST)","value":"21.6% improvement over baseline"},{"metric":"Inference speed (Llama-2-Chat 70B + NEST)","value":"1.8x speedup"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of NEST, a novel semi-parametric language modeling approach that incorporates real-world text spans of arbitrary length into LM generations and provides attribution to their sources.","NEST performs token-level retrieval at each inference step to compute a semi-parametric mixture distribution and identify promising span continuations.","Use of a novel Relative Retrieval Confidence (RRC) score for dynamic interpolation between LM and retrieval outputs.","Dynamic span selection and relaxed speculative decoding to accept multi-token spans, improving both fluency and efficiency.","NEST achieves both higher generation quality and attribution rate than kNN-LM, and is competitive with in-context retrieval augmentation, while being significantly faster."]}

methods: Nearest Neighbor Speculative Decoding (NEST)
Confidence-based interpolation using Relative Retrieval Confidence (RRC) score
Dynamic span selection inspired by Copy Generator (COG)
Relaxed speculative decoding with prefix acceptance
problems: Hallucination in large language models (LLMs)
Lack of attribution in LLM generations
Slow inference speeds and non-fluent text in semi-parametric LMs like kNN-LM
Inefficiency and unreliable attribution in retrieval-augmented LMs
novelty_claims: Introduction of NEST, a novel semi-parametric language modeling approach that incorporates real-world text spans of arbitrary length into LM generations and provides attribution to their sources.
NEST performs token-level retrieval at each inference step to compute a semi-parametric mixture distribution and identify promising span continuations.
Use of a novel Relative Retrieval Confidence (RRC) score for dynamic interpolation between LM and retrieval outputs.
Dynamic span selection and relaxed speculative decoding to accept multi-token spans, improving both fluency and efficiency.
NEST achieves both higher generation quality and attribution rate than kNN-LM, and is competitive with in-context retrieval augmentation, while being significantly faster.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["REPLUG: a retrieval-augmented language modeling framework that treats the language model as a black box and augments it with a tuneable retrieval model by prepending retrieved documents to the input","REPLUG LSR: a training scheme where the retriever is tuned using supervision signals from the black-box language model, preferring documents that improve LM perplexity","Ensemble scheme: encodes retrieved documents in parallel with the same black-box LM to trade compute for accuracy"],"problems":["How to augment large black-box language models (e.g., GPT-3, Codex) with retrieval when internal representations and fine-tuning are not accessible","Reducing hallucination and increasing knowledge coverage in large language models","Improving language modeling and in-context learning performance of large LMs using retrieval augmentation"],"datasets":["MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding)","Natural Questions (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019)","TriviaQA (Joshi et al., 2017)"],"metrics":["Language modeling perplexity","MMLU accuracy","Open-domain QA accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"GPT-3 (175B) language modeling improvement","value":"6.3% increase"},{"metric":"Codex (175B) five-shot MMLU accuracy improvement","value":"5.1% increase"},{"metric":"Codex (175B) MMLU accuracy improvement","value":"4.5% increase"}],"novelty_claims":["REPLUG is the first retrieval-augmented language modeling framework designed for large black-box language models, requiring no access to internal representations or fine-tuning.","The retriever is adapted to the language model using supervision from the LM's output, rather than adapting the LM to the retriever.","REPLUG can be applied to any existing language model, including those only accessible via API, making it highly flexible and practical.","Demonstrates for the first time the benefits of retrieval augmentation for very large LMs (>100B parameters) in both reducing perplexity and improving in-context learning."]}

methods: REPLUG: a retrieval-augmented language modeling framework that treats the language model as a black box and augments it with a tuneable retrieval model by prepending retrieved documents to the input
REPLUG LSR: a training scheme where the retriever is tuned using supervision signals from the black-box language model, preferring documents that improve LM perplexity
Ensemble scheme: encodes retrieved documents in parallel with the same black-box LM to trade compute for accuracy
problems: How to augment large black-box language models (e.g., GPT-3, Codex) with retrieval when internal representations and fine-tuning are not accessible
Reducing hallucination and increasing knowledge coverage in large language models
Improving language modeling and in-context learning performance of large LMs using retrieval augmentation
novelty_claims: REPLUG is the first retrieval-augmented language modeling framework designed for large black-box language models, requiring no access to internal representations or fine-tuning.
The retriever is adapted to the language model using supervision from the LM's output, rather than adapting the LM to the retriever.
REPLUG can be applied to any existing language model, including those only accessible via API, making it highly flexible and practical.
Demonstrates for the first time the benefits of retrieval augmentation for very large LMs (>100B parameters) in both reducing perplexity and improving in-context learning.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Retrieval-Augmented Language Model (REALM) pre-training","Unsupervised pre-training of a knowledge retriever using masked language modeling as the learning signal","End-to-end training with backpropagation through a retrieval step over millions of documents","Retriever structured for efficient Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS)"],"problems":["Implicit storage of world knowledge in language model parameters, leading to scalability and interpretability issues","Need for more modular and interpretable knowledge integration in language models","Improving performance on open-domain question answering (Open-QA) tasks"],"datasets":["NATURALQUESTIONS-OPEN","WEBQUESTIONS","CURATEDTREC"],"metrics":["Accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy improvement over previous state-of-the-art on Open-QA benchmarks","value":"4-16% absolute accuracy increase"}],"novelty_claims":["First to pre-train a knowledge retriever in an unsupervised manner using masked language modeling and backpropagation through retrieval over millions of documents","REALM augments language model pre-training with a learned retriever, enabling explicit, modular, and interpretable access to world knowledge","REALM achieves new state-of-the-art results on three Open-QA benchmarks, outperforming both implicit and explicit knowledge storage models","REALM's retriever is designed to transfer to downstream tasks, unlike previous retrieval-augmented models"]}

methods: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model (REALM) pre-training
Unsupervised pre-training of a knowledge retriever using masked language modeling as the learning signal
End-to-end training with backpropagation through a retrieval step over millions of documents
Retriever structured for efficient Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS)
problems: Implicit storage of world knowledge in language model parameters, leading to scalability and interpretability issues
Need for more modular and interpretable knowledge integration in language models
Improving performance on open-domain question answering (Open-QA) tasks

results.metric: Accuracy improvement over previous state-of-the-art on Open-QA benchmarks

novelty_claims: First to pre-train a knowledge retriever in an unsupervised manner using masked language modeling and backpropagation through retrieval over millions of documents
REALM augments language model pre-training with a learned retriever, enabling explicit, modular, and interpretable access to world knowledge
REALM achieves new state-of-the-art results on three Open-QA benchmarks, outperforming both implicit and explicit knowledge storage models
REALM's retriever is designed to transfer to downstream tasks, unlike previous retrieval-augmented models

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Forward-Looking Active REtrieval augmented generation (FLARE): an active retrieval method that anticipates future content by generating a temporary next sentence, uses it as a query to retrieve relevant documents if it contains low-confidence tokens, and then regenerates the sentence conditioning on the retrieved documents; this process is repeated iteratively throughout generation.","Active retrieval strategy: retrieve only when the language model generates low-probability (low-confidence) tokens, indicating a lack of knowledge."],"problems":["Hallucination and factual inaccuracy in large language model (LM) outputs.","Limitations of single-time retrieval augmented LMs for long-form, knowledge-intensive generation tasks.","Need for continual and contextually relevant information retrieval during long-form text generation."],"datasets":["2WikiMultihopQA (multihop QA)","StrategyQA (commonsense reasoning)","ASQA (long-form QA)","WikiAsp (open-domain summarization)"],"metrics":["Performance metrics for long-form knowledge-intensive generation tasks (exact metrics not specified in the provided content, but likely include factual accuracy, ROUGE, BLEU, or similar)"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance on 2WikiMultihopQA, StrategyQA, ASQA, WikiAsp","value":"FLARE achieves superior or competitive performance compared to single-time and multi-time retrieval baselines on all tasks."}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a generalized view of active retrieval augmented generation, where the model actively decides when and what to retrieve throughout the generation process.","Introduces FLARE, a generic method that anticipates future content to guide retrieval, rather than relying solely on past context or fixed intervals.","FLARE retrieves only when the model is uncertain (low-confidence tokens), reducing unnecessary retrievals and improving relevance.","FLARE is applicable to any existing language model at inference time without additional training.","Demonstrates effectiveness and generalizability of FLARE across diverse long-form, knowledge-intensive generation tasks."]}

methods: Forward-Looking Active REtrieval augmented generation (FLARE): an active retrieval method that anticipates future content by generating a temporary next sentence, uses it as a query to retrieve relevant documents if it contains low-confidence tokens, and then regenerates the sentence conditioning on the retrieved documents; this process is repeated iteratively throughout generation.
Active retrieval strategy: retrieve only when the language model generates low-probability (low-confidence) tokens, indicating a lack of knowledge.
problems: Hallucination and factual inaccuracy in large language model (LM) outputs.
Limitations of single-time retrieval augmented LMs for long-form, knowledge-intensive generation tasks.
Need for continual and contextually relevant information retrieval during long-form text generation.
metrics: Performance metrics for long-form knowledge-intensive generation tasks (exact metrics not specified in the provided content, but likely include factual accuracy, ROUGE, BLEU, or similar)

results.value: FLARE achieves superior or competitive performance compared to single-time and multi-time retrieval baselines on all tasks.

novelty_claims: Proposes a generalized view of active retrieval augmented generation, where the model actively decides when and what to retrieve throughout the generation process.
Introduces FLARE, a generic method that anticipates future content to guide retrieval, rather than relying solely on past context or fixed intervals.
FLARE retrieves only when the model is uncertain (low-confidence tokens), reducing unnecessary retrievals and improving relevance.
FLARE is applicable to any existing language model at inference time without additional training.
Demonstrates effectiveness and generalizability of FLARE across diverse long-form, knowledge-intensive generation tasks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Adaptive retrieval using a lightweight predictive network to decide when to query the datastore","Datastore pruning via clustering","Datastore pruning via importance-guided filtering","Datastore pruning via greedy merging","Datastore compression through vector dimensionality reduction"],"problems":["High inference overhead of non-parametric neural language models due to large-scale nearest neighbor retrieval at test time","Inefficiency of k-nearest neighbors language models (kNN-LM) in practical deployment scenarios"],"datasets":["WikiText-103","Domain-adaptation datasets (not specifically named)"],"metrics":["Perplexity","Inference speed"],"results":[{"metric":"Inference speed-up","value":"up to 6x faster than baseline kNN-LM"},{"metric":"Perplexity","value":"Comparable to kNN-LM baseline"}],"novelty_claims":["First comprehensive exploration of efficiency improvements for kNN-LM along three dimensions: adaptive retrieval, datastore pruning, and vector dimensionality reduction","Demonstration that adaptive retrieval can automatically prune unnecessary retrieval operations without significant loss in performance","Empirical evidence that simple dimension reduction techniques can improve both speed and performance of non-parametric LMs","Presentation of practical methods for making non-parametric LMs more deployable in real-world settings by significantly reducing inference overhead"]}

methods: Adaptive retrieval using a lightweight predictive network to decide when to query the datastore
Datastore pruning via clustering
Datastore pruning via importance-guided filtering
Datastore pruning via greedy merging
Datastore compression through vector dimensionality reduction
problems: High inference overhead of non-parametric neural language models due to large-scale nearest neighbor retrieval at test time
Inefficiency of k-nearest neighbors language models (kNN-LM) in practical deployment scenarios

novelty_claims: First comprehensive exploration of efficiency improvements for kNN-LM along three dimensions: adaptive retrieval, datastore pruning, and vector dimensionality reduction
Demonstration that adaptive retrieval can automatically prune unnecessary retrieval operations without significant loss in performance
Empirical evidence that simple dimension reduction techniques can improve both speed and performance of non-parametric LMs
Presentation of practical methods for making non-parametric LMs more deployable in real-world settings by significantly reducing inference overhead

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Retrieval-Based Speculative Decoding (REST): a method that uses retrieval from a datastore (built from pretraining or instruction-tuning corpora) to generate draft tokens for speculative decoding, replacing the need for a small draft language model; uses Trie construction and frequency-based selection for draft tokens; employs a tree attention mask for efficient verification by the LLM"],"problems":["High inference cost and inefficiency in autoregressive generation with large language models (LLMs) due to repeated memory loading and computation","Difficulty in obtaining and integrating high-quality draft models for speculative decoding, which often require additional training and maintenance"],"datasets":["The Stack (Python subset, 2.7M samples) for code domain","UltraChat (774K conversations) for general domain","HumanEval (for code generation evaluation)","MT-Bench (for general text generation evaluation)"],"metrics":["Speedup (generation acceleration factor)"],"results":[{"metric":"Speedup on CodeLlama 7B/13B (HumanEval)","value":"2.12× to 2.36×"},{"metric":"Speedup on Vicuna 7B/13B (MT-Bench)","value":"1.62× to 1.77×"}],"novelty_claims":["REST is the first speculative decoding method to use retrieval from a data corpus instead of a parametric draft language model, eliminating the need for additional model training.","REST is plug-and-play and can accelerate any LLM without requiring changes to the model or extra training.","REST leverages a non-parametric retrieval datastore, making it easy to port and integrate with different LLMs.","REST achieves significant speedup in both code and general text generation tasks."]}

methods: Retrieval-Based Speculative Decoding (REST): a method that uses retrieval from a datastore (built from pretraining or instruction-tuning corpora) to generate draft tokens for speculative decoding, replacing the need for a small draft language model; uses Trie construction and frequency-based selection for draft tokens; employs a tree attention mask for efficient verification by the LLM
problems: High inference cost and inefficiency in autoregressive generation with large language models (LLMs) due to repeated memory loading and computation
Difficulty in obtaining and integrating high-quality draft models for speculative decoding, which often require additional training and maintenance
datasets: The Stack (Python subset, 2.7M samples) for code domain
novelty_claims: REST is the first speculative decoding method to use retrieval from a data corpus instead of a parametric draft language model, eliminating the need for additional model training.
REST is plug-and-play and can accelerate any LLM without requiring changes to the model or extra training.
REST leverages a non-parametric retrieval datastore, making it easy to port and integrate with different LLMs.
REST achieves significant speedup in both code and general text generation tasks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["kNN-LM: Extending a pre-trained neural language model by linearly interpolating its next word distribution with a k-nearest neighbors (kNN) model, where neighbors are computed in the LM embedding space and can be drawn from any text collection"],"problems":["Improving language model performance, especially on rare patterns and long-tail phenomena","Efficiently scaling language models to larger training sets without retraining","Effective domain adaptation for language models without additional training"],"datasets":["Wikitext-103","3-billion token dataset (unspecified, used for scaling experiments)"],"metrics":["Perplexity"],"results":[{"metric":"Perplexity (Wikitext-103)","value":"15.79 (state-of-the-art, 2.86 point improvement over base model)"},{"metric":"Perplexity (scaling experiment)","value":"Model trained on 100M tokens + kNN search over 3B tokens outperforms model trained on all 3B tokens"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes kNN-LM, a method that augments pre-trained LMs with a k-nearest neighbor retrieval mechanism in the embedding space, allowing explicit memorization of rare patterns","Achieves state-of-the-art perplexity on Wikitext-103 without additional training","Demonstrates efficient scaling and domain adaptation by varying the nearest neighbor datastore, again without retraining","Shows that learning similarity between text sequences (representation learning) is easier than next-word prediction, and that nearest neighbor search is effective for language modeling in the long tail"]}

methods: kNN-LM: Extending a pre-trained neural language model by linearly interpolating its next word distribution with a k-nearest neighbors (kNN) model, where neighbors are computed in the LM embedding space and can be drawn from any text collection
problems: Improving language model performance, especially on rare patterns and long-tail phenomena
Efficiently scaling language models to larger training sets without retraining
Effective domain adaptation for language models without additional training
datasets: 3-billion token dataset (unspecified, used for scaling experiments)

results.value: Model trained on 100M tokens + kNN search over 3B tokens outperforms model trained on all 3B tokens

novelty_claims: Proposes kNN-LM, a method that augments pre-trained LMs with a k-nearest neighbor retrieval mechanism in the embedding space, allowing explicit memorization of rare patterns
Achieves state-of-the-art perplexity on Wikitext-103 without additional training
Demonstrates efficient scaling and domain adaptation by varying the nearest neighbor datastore, again without retraining

Shows that learning similarity between text sequences (representation learning) is easier than next-word prediction, and that nearest neighbor search is effective for language modeling in the long tail

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Analysis of lexical and semantic matching in kNN-LM retrievals","Adaptive kNN-LM: dynamically assigning the interpolation coefficient based on retrieval quality (semantic similarity)","Empirical evaluation of adaptive kNN-LM on language modeling tasks"],"problems":["Understanding when and how retrieval improves kNN-LM performance","Determining the impact of lexical and semantic similarity between queries and retrieved items in kNN-LM","Improving the effectiveness of retrieval-augmented language models by leveraging retrieval quality"],"datasets":["Wikitext-103","PG-19"],"metrics":["Perplexity"],"results":[{"metric":"Perplexity (Wikitext-103, vanilla kNN-LM)","value":"16.12"},{"metric":"Perplexity (Wikitext-103, base LM)","value":"18.65"},{"metric":"Perplexity improvement (Wikitext-103, adaptive kNN-LM over vanilla kNN-LM)","value":"~4% improvement"}],"novelty_claims":["First detailed analysis of the role of lexical and semantic similarity in kNN-LM retrievals and their impact on performance.","Introduction of an adaptive kNN-LM that dynamically adjusts the interpolation coefficient based on retrieval quality (semantic similarity).","Demonstration that semantic similarity is a strong predictor of kNN-LM performance and can be used to improve retrieval-augmented LMs.","Empirical evidence that the adaptive kNN-LM formulation yields significant perplexity improvements over the vanilla kNN-LM on standard language modeling benchmarks."]}

methods: Analysis of lexical and semantic matching in kNN-LM retrievals
Adaptive kNN-LM: dynamically assigning the interpolation coefficient based on retrieval quality (semantic similarity)
Empirical evaluation of adaptive kNN-LM on language modeling tasks
problems: Understanding when and how retrieval improves kNN-LM performance
Determining the impact of lexical and semantic similarity between queries and retrieved items in kNN-LM
Improving the effectiveness of retrieval-augmented language models by leveraging retrieval quality

results.metric: Perplexity improvement (Wikitext-103, adaptive kNN-LM over vanilla kNN-LM)

novelty_claims: First detailed analysis of the role of lexical and semantic similarity in kNN-LM retrievals and their impact on performance.
Introduction of an adaptive kNN-LM that dynamically adjusts the interpolation coefficient based on retrieval quality (semantic similarity).
Demonstration that semantic similarity is a strong predictor of kNN-LM performance and can be used to improve retrieval-augmented LMs.
Empirical evidence that the adaptive kNN-LM formulation yields significant perplexity improvements over the vanilla kNN-LM on standard language modeling benchmarks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Retrieval-Enhanced Transformer (RETRO): an autoregressive language model augmented with retrieval from a massive text database","Chunked cross-attention mechanism to incorporate retrieved text into predictions","Use of a frozen BERT retriever for efficient retrieval without retriever training","Chunking input sequences and retrieving similar text chunks based on local similarity","RETROfitting: augmenting pre-trained transformers with retrieval capabilities"],"problems":["Improving language model performance without increasing model parameters or compute","Scaling retrieval-augmented language models to trillions of tokens","Addressing test set leakage in evaluation of retrieval-augmented models","Enhancing knowledge-intensive downstream tasks (e.g., question answering) with retrieval"],"datasets":["The Pile","Wikitext103","C4 (Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus)"],"metrics":["Perplexity","Downstream task accuracy (e.g., question answering)"],"results":[{"metric":"Perplexity on The Pile","value":"Comparable to GPT-3 and Jurassic-1 with 25x fewer parameters"},{"metric":"Performance on Wikitext103 and The Pile","value":"State-of-the-art results with largest RETRO model"},{"metric":"Performance scaling","value":"Constant gain across model sizes (150M to 7B parameters); gain increases with database and number of retrieved neighbors"}],"novelty_claims":["First to demonstrate benefits of scaling retrieval databases to trillions of tokens for large language models","Introduction of RETRO, a retrieval-augmented transformer that achieves comparable performance to much larger models with fewer parameters","Demonstration that retrieval-augmented models can be fine-tuned for downstream tasks and can be retrofitted to pre-trained transformers","Proposal of an evaluation methodology to address test set leakage in retrieval-augmented models"]}

methods: Retrieval-Enhanced Transformer (RETRO): an autoregressive language model augmented with retrieval from a massive text database
Chunked cross-attention mechanism to incorporate retrieved text into predictions
Use of a frozen BERT retriever for efficient retrieval without retriever training
Chunking input sequences and retrieving similar text chunks based on local similarity
RETROfitting: augmenting pre-trained transformers with retrieval capabilities
problems: Improving language model performance without increasing model parameters or compute
Scaling retrieval-augmented language models to trillions of tokens
Addressing test set leakage in evaluation of retrieval-augmented models
Enhancing knowledge-intensive downstream tasks (e.g., question answering) with retrieval

results.value: Comparable to GPT-3 and Jurassic-1 with 25x fewer parameters

results.metric: Performance on Wikitext103 and The Pile
results.value: State-of-the-art results with largest RETRO model
Constant gain across model sizes (150M to 7B parameters); gain increases with database and number of retrieved neighbors

novelty_claims: First to demonstrate benefits of scaling retrieval databases to trillions of tokens for large language models
Introduction of RETRO, a retrieval-augmented transformer that achieves comparable performance to much larger models with fewer parameters
Demonstration that retrieval-augmented models can be fine-tuned for downstream tasks and can be retrofitted to pre-trained transformers
Proposal of an evaluation methodology to address test set leakage in retrieval-augmented models

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["COG (COPY-GENERATOR): a method that formulates text generation as a sequence of copy-and-paste operations from an indexed collection of text segments (phrases), using contextualized vector representations and efficient vector search for retrieval"],"problems":["Improving the quality of text generation beyond traditional token-level autoregressive models","Enabling efficient and accurate domain adaptation for text generation without retraining","Reducing the number of decoding steps in text generation by generating multi-word phrases at once"],"datasets":["WikiText-103","Law-MT","En-Wiki"],"metrics":["MAUVE","Human preference"],"results":[{"metric":"MAUVE (WikiText-103)","value":"26.14 (COG) vs. 23.43 (baseline)"},{"metric":"Human preference (WikiText-103)","value":"48% (COG) vs. 28% (baseline)"},{"metric":"MAUVE (Law-MT, domain adaptation)","value":"28.14 (COG) vs. 26.85 (baseline)"},{"metric":"Human preference (Law-MT, domain adaptation)","value":"52% (COG) vs. 36% (baseline)"},{"metric":"MAUVE (En-Wiki, scaling up collection)","value":"26.97 (COG) vs. 23.43 (baseline)"}],"novelty_claims":["Reformulating text generation as a sequence of copy-and-paste operations from an indexed collection of text segments, rather than token-level prediction from a fixed vocabulary","Allowing generation of multi-word phrases in a single step, reducing decoding steps and improving inference efficiency","Enabling training-free domain adaptation and scaling to larger text collections by simply switching the underlying text collection without further training","Demonstrating that this approach can outperform standard neural language model baselines on both automatic and human evaluation metrics"]}

methods: COG (COPY-GENERATOR): a method that formulates text generation as a sequence of copy-and-paste operations from an indexed collection of text segments (phrases), using contextualized vector representations and efficient vector search for retrieval
problems: Improving the quality of text generation beyond traditional token-level autoregressive models
Enabling efficient and accurate domain adaptation for text generation without retraining
Reducing the number of decoding steps in text generation by generating multi-word phrases at once
novelty_claims: Reformulating text generation as a sequence of copy-and-paste operations from an indexed collection of text segments, rather than token-level prediction from a fixed vocabulary
Allowing generation of multi-word phrases in a single step, reducing decoding steps and improving inference efficiency
Enabling training-free domain adaptation and scaling to larger text collections by simply switching the underlying text collection without further training
Demonstrating that this approach can outperform standard neural language model baselines on both automatic and human evaluation metrics

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Retrieval-augmented code completion framework (ReACC)","Hybrid code retriever combining dense (dual-encoder, GraphCodeBERT-initialized) and sparse (BM25/TF-IDF) retrieval","Stage-wise training: pre-train retriever with contrastive learning and data augmentation, then train generator","Partial code-to-code search task for retrieval of similar code snippets","Auto-regressive language model (CodeGPT-adapted) as code completion generator","Semantic-preserving transformations for data augmentation"],"problems":["Limited context in code completion models (only internal file/project context)","Lack of utilization of external code snippets for code completion","Need for effective retrieval of semantically and lexically similar code for code completion","Scarcity of labeled similar code pairs for training retrievers"],"datasets":["CodeXGLUE (Python and Java)","CodeNet (Python and Java)"],"metrics":["Code completion accuracy (exact match)","State-of-the-art (SOTA) performance comparison"],"results":[{"metric":"Code completion accuracy (CodeXGLUE, Python)","value":"State-of-the-art (exact value not specified)"},{"metric":"Code completion accuracy (CodeXGLUE, Java)","value":"State-of-the-art (exact value not specified)"},{"metric":"Code completion accuracy (CodeNet, Python)","value":"State-of-the-art (exact value not specified)"},{"metric":"Code completion accuracy (CodeNet, Java)","value":"State-of-the-art (exact value not specified)"}],"novelty_claims":["First to propose a retrieval-augmented code completion framework leveraging both lexical and semantic similarity from external code snippets.","Introduction of the partial code-to-code search task for code retrieval in code completion.","Use of semantic-preserving transformations for data augmentation in pre-training the code retriever.","Demonstration that external code retrieval significantly improves code completion accuracy over prior methods."]}

methods: Retrieval-augmented code completion framework (ReACC)
Hybrid code retriever combining dense (dual-encoder, GraphCodeBERT-initialized) and sparse (BM25/TF-IDF) retrieval
Stage-wise training: pre-train retriever with contrastive learning and data augmentation, then train generator
Partial code-to-code search task for retrieval of similar code snippets
Auto-regressive language model (CodeGPT-adapted) as code completion generator
Semantic-preserving transformations for data augmentation
problems: Limited context in code completion models (only internal file/project context)
Lack of utilization of external code snippets for code completion
Need for effective retrieval of semantically and lexically similar code for code completion
Scarcity of labeled similar code pairs for training retrievers
novelty_claims: First to propose a retrieval-augmented code completion framework leveraging both lexical and semantic similarity from external code snippets.
Introduction of the partial code-to-code search task for code retrieval in code completion.
Use of semantic-preserving transformations for data augmentation in pre-training the code retriever.
Demonstration that external code retrieval significantly improves code completion accuracy over prior methods.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Unified Generation-Retrieval framework for Image Captioning (UGRIC)","Adversarial learning to combine generation and retrieval approaches","Leveraging N-best response candidates from retrieval-based models","Copying mechanism to select words from retrieved candidate captions"],"problems":["Limitations of generation-based and retrieval-based image captioning methods","Improving informativeness and quality of generated image captions"],"datasets":["MSCOCO"],"metrics":["BLEU","METEOR","ROUGE","CIDEr"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a unified framework (UGRIC) that combines generation and retrieval-based image captioning using adversarial learning.","Leverages N-best response candidates from retrieval-based models to enhance generation-based captioning.","Introduces a copying mechanism to select and insert words from retrieved captions into generated captions to improve informativeness."]}

methods: Unified Generation-Retrieval framework for Image Captioning (UGRIC)
Adversarial learning to combine generation and retrieval approaches
Leveraging N-best response candidates from retrieval-based models
Copying mechanism to select words from retrieved candidate captions
problems: Limitations of generation-based and retrieval-based image captioning methods
Improving informativeness and quality of generated image captions
novelty_claims: Proposes a unified framework (UGRIC) that combines generation and retrieval-based image captioning using adversarial learning.
Leverages N-best response candidates from retrieval-based models to enhance generation-based captioning.
Introduces a copying mechanism to select and insert words from retrieved captions into generated captions to improve informativeness.

methods: Atlas: a retrieval-augmented language model using a dense retriever (dual-encoder architecture based on Contriever) and a sequence-to-sequence model with Fusion-in-Decoder architecture
Joint pre-training of retriever and language model components
Fine-tuning strategies for both retriever and language model
Use of compressed document index via product quantization for memory efficiency
problems: Few-shot learning for knowledge-intensive tasks (e.g., question answering, fact checking)
Reducing the need for massive parameter counts in language models for knowledge storage
Improving sample efficiency and adaptability of language models in few-shot settings
Demonstrating updatability and interpretability of retrieval-augmented models

results.metric: Accuracy improvement over previous SOTA (NaturalQuestions, full dataset)
Accuracy improvement over previous SOTA (TriviaQA, full dataset)

novelty_claims: First demonstration that retrieval-augmented language models can achieve strong few-shot learning performance on knowledge-intensive tasks, outperforming much larger LLMs.
Atlas achieves few-shot abilities at lower parameter scale than standard LLMs, decoupling memorization from generalization.
Joint pre-training of retriever and language model is crucial for few-shot performance.
Atlas sets new state-of-the-art results on several knowledge-intensive tasks in both few-shot and full-dataset settings.
Demonstrates that compressed document indices can achieve comparable performance to uncompressed indices with significant memory savings.
Shows that the document index can be easily updated, enhancing adaptability.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Proxy-tuning: a lightweight decoding-time algorithm that tunes large black-box language models by using the difference in predictions between a small tuned model (expert) and its untuned version (anti-expert) to shift the output distribution of the large model, without accessing its internal weights.","Application of DEXPERTS (Decoding-time Experts Equation) to combine the guidance from the expert and anti-expert with the base model's predictions.","Optional introduction of a hyperparameter to control the amount of guidance at runtime."],"problems":["Efficiently adapting large pretrained language models to desired behaviors without access to their internal weights (black-box setting).","Reducing the resource intensity of tuning large language models.","Customizing proprietary or private LMs for specific tasks, domains, or instruction-following when direct finetuning is not possible."],"datasets":["TruthfulQA","GSM (Cobbe et al., 2021)","Toxigen (Hartvigsen et al., 2022)","Knowledge, reasoning, and safety benchmarks (unspecified, but referenced as evaluation domains)","Coding benchmarks (for code domain adaptation; specific datasets not named)","Question-answering and math problem datasets (unspecified)"],"metrics":["Performance gap closed (relative to directly tuned models)","Absolute improvement (percentage points) on benchmarks","Accuracy (for temporal adaptation and knowledge tasks)"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance gap closed (Llama2-70B vs Llama2-70B-Chat)","value":"88%"},{"metric":"Performance gap closed (Llama2-13B vs Llama2-13B-Chat)","value":"91%"},{"metric":"Absolute improvement on coding benchmarks (Llama2-13B with Codellama-7B proxy)","value":"17-32%"},{"metric":"Absolute improvement on QA and math tasks (Llama2-70B)","value":"31% over untuned 70B, 9% over tuned 7B task model"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of proxy-tuning, a decoding-time algorithm that enables tuning of large black-box LMs using only their output distributions, not their weights.","Demonstration that proxy-tuning can close most of the performance gap between untuned and directly tuned large models using only small, cheap-to-tune models as proxies.","Application of proxy-tuning to a truly black-box commercial model (GPT-3.5) for temporal adaptation, even with limited access to output probabilities.","Proxy-tuning allows for efficient, effective customization of large, potentially proprietary LMs, enabling adaptation without compromising model privacy."]}

methods: Proxy-tuning: a lightweight decoding-time algorithm that tunes large black-box language models by using the difference in predictions between a small tuned model (expert) and its untuned version (anti-expert) to shift the output distribution of the large model, without accessing its internal weights.
Application of DEXPERTS (Decoding-time Experts Equation) to combine the guidance from the expert and anti-expert with the base model's predictions.
Optional introduction of a hyperparameter to control the amount of guidance at runtime.
problems: Efficiently adapting large pretrained language models to desired behaviors without access to their internal weights (black-box setting).
Reducing the resource intensity of tuning large language models.
Customizing proprietary or private LMs for specific tasks, domains, or instruction-following when direct finetuning is not possible.
datasets: Knowledge, reasoning, and safety benchmarks (unspecified, but referenced as evaluation domains)
Coding benchmarks (for code domain adaptation; specific datasets not named)
metrics: Performance gap closed (relative to directly tuned models)
Accuracy (for temporal adaptation and knowledge tasks)

results.metric: Absolute improvement on coding benchmarks (Llama2-13B with Codellama-7B proxy)
Absolute improvement on QA and math tasks (Llama2-70B)
results.value: 31% over untuned 70B, 9% over tuned 7B task model

novelty_claims: Introduction of proxy-tuning, a decoding-time algorithm that enables tuning of large black-box LMs using only their output distributions, not their weights.
Demonstration that proxy-tuning can close most of the performance gap between untuned and directly tuned large models using only small, cheap-to-tune models as proxies.
Application of proxy-tuning to a truly black-box commercial model (GPT-3.5) for temporal adaptation, even with limited access to output probabilities.
Proxy-tuning allows for efficient, effective customization of large, potentially proprietary LMs, enabling adaptation without compromising model privacy.
